name: Monoio
summary: Bytedance's Rust thread-per-core io_uring runtime; same design family as Glommio with each worker bound to a CPU core running its own io_uring loop and futures pinned to a single core for their lifetime.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Monoio** (Bytedance) is the second canonical Rust thread-per-core `io_uring` runtime, alongside Glommio (`data/glommio`). Each worker thread runs its own independent single-threaded event loop bound to a specific CPU core via affinity, with its own `io_uring` instance for asynchronous I/O. Inter-core coordination is by explicit message passing through bounded channels rather than work-stealing across a shared scheduler.

Futures are pinned to one core for their lifetime, eliminating cross-core synchronisation on the hot path. Distinct from Tokio's M:N work-stealing model where futures freely migrate between worker threads.

sections.title: Design family and production use
sections.content:
Monoio shares Glommio's design point: thread-per-core ownership, no work-stealing, per-core `io_uring` rings, message-passing for cross-core coordination. The trade-off is identical — perfect cache locality and zero shared-state contention, at the cost of needing to manually balance work across cores.

Production use: Bytedance internal services (TikTok-adjacent infrastructure), proxies, and high-throughput edge services. Status (as of 2026-04): Apache-2.0 licensed, actively maintained. The existence of two independent thread-per-core runtimes from independent vendors (Datadog, Bytedance) is itself the data point: the design has converged across the industry for the latency-and-throughput-dominant workloads where work-stealing's cross-core synchronisation cost shows up in tail latency.
